words: LILLIAN: But I livedhunfil I was through college and then I went
away to teach. And it was fifteen years before he and I were married.
his He went away in the meantime to New York City and got a PhD and worked
in an institution for the mentally retarded and went into the Navy and
all that so.
SAM SCHRAGER: Had you known each other well in high school?

speaker: LO
words: Well we had dated off and on all through high school and college, but

speaker: LO
words: it was just one of those things that we got together eventually.

speaker: LO
words: but it was a long time. And of course I had been independent for a

speaker: LO
words: long time, and the girls that were interviewing me were interested in

speaker: LO
words: that. And oh, what adjustments I had to make from being independent

speaker: LO
words: and...

speaker: SS
words: To getting married?

speaker: LO
words: Yes, to marrying and being a housewife with no job, no

speaker: LO
words: outside committments and so on.

speaker: SS
words: curious about some of that too. I think that the idea of

speaker: SS
words: not patting married was pretty unusual, wasn't it? Of not getting married

speaker: SS
words: as soon as you got grown?

speaker: LO
words: Oh y«ah, yes. I mean it was just expected. And I can remember when I was

speaker: LO
words: teaching that one of my friends who was a teacher left her job to be

speaker: LO
words: married at that time.nAnd I thought it was high time she was twenty-six!

speaker: LO
words: I thought, 'She just better cause she'll be an old maid now if she doesn't

speaker: LO
words: look out.

speaker: SS

words: for an independent career? And did you yourself want to get married?

speaker: LO
words: Oh I think so, I think so. I think it was, you know, it was the accepted

speaker: LO
words: thing but when you wait that long you get to the point where all

speaker: LO
words: are the interesting men your age already married. And you just don't meet

speaker: LO
words: very many eligibles. And I felt that I wouldn't be satisfied to marry

speaker: LO
words: somebody that didn't have pretty much the same educational and intellectual

speaker: LO
words: background that I had had. I just didn't think it would make a good

speaker: LO
words: marriage So it was quite a while.

speaker: SS
words: Where did you teach?

speaker: LO
words: Oh, I taught in Klamath Falls, Oregon. And then I taught at what is now

speaker: LO
words: ISU, at that time it was

speaker: LO
words: southern branch of the University of Idaho. And I taught there until

speaker: LO
words: during the war I worked for the USO for about oh, about a year and a half,

speaker: LO
words: in Texas and Salt Lake Gity. And then I went back to my teahing job.

speaker: LO
words: So I was mainly teaching.

speaker: SS
words: When you were teaching were your friends mostly married or were they

speaker: SS
words: mostly single women?

speaker: LO

speaker: LO
words: women on that campus. And it was a rather sociable group. We did lots

speaker: LO
words: of things together. And that is something that I have missed all my

speaker: LO
words: married life. I just didn't have friends of that particular kind as much

speaker: LO
words: as I did then.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: you towards the alter?

speaker: LO
words: No, no. She wasn't the kind that was pushing me toward it. I think she was

speaker: LO
words: very happy when I married Ott because he had her full approval. But

speaker: LO
words: I had an aunt that was just a storybook mother that was trying to marry

speaker: LO
words: her daughter off. And she lived in Spokane, and when we used to visit her,

speaker: LO
words: why a great deal of talk went into that bout her daughter and her dates

speaker: LO
words: she could have married this one if she had played her cards right

speaker: LO
words: and so on. There was great preoccupatdon on her part, not so with my

speaker: LO
words: mother.

speaker: SS
words: When my mother was growing up she's about sixty now, she's told

speaker: SS
words: me about her high school days and after. The attitudes I that that had about

speaker: SS
words: love, just I find incredible to hear her talk about them. I mean it sounded

speaker: SS
words: like, at least in the milie^which I think was pretty typical, the one that

speaker: SS
words: my mother was growing up in, you know, people were always talking about

speaker: SS
words: getting married and getting engaged, and these things were done very lightly.

speaker: SS
words: And as my mother told me, it's an old saw but people seem to be in

speaker: SS
words: love with being in love.

speaker: LO
words: Um hum.

speaker: SS
words: And actually it seemed fairly shallow, I mean, at one point be engaged to

speaker: SS
words: somebody and be going out with his rSmmate on the side and that kind of

speaker: SS
words: thing.

speaker: LO
words: Um hum.

speaker: SS
words: I've always found that puzzling, but interesting. In some ways it seemed

speaker: SS
words: like there was a lot more of a dating game than thereks now.

speaker: LO
words: Oh, I think so. And I think that it is on the whole a much healthier

speaker: LO
words: attitude. that women don't feel that they're second class citizens

speaker: LO
words: if they're not married anymore. If they're single from choice, that they

speaker: LO
words: don't have to make any excuses for it. I think this is much better. One of

speaker: LO
words: the things that those girls were interested in was my comment that when I

speaker: LO
words: was in college very few married women were in school. And they just couldn't

speaker: LO
words: understand that. But I don't suppose that at the time I was in college

speaker: LO
words: that I knew more than half a dozen. And we thought they were sort of queer,

speaker: LO
words: when married wmen were going to college. Well of course now, it's so widely

speaker: LO
words: accepted, nobody regardless of age would feel strange in a college

speaker: LO
words: classromm, I think, or would be made to feel that he didn't belong.

speaker: LO
words: But at that time college students were all, you know, people in their teens

speaker: LO
words: and twenties. But this really was a puzzle to the girls.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, it sounds like the same way to me. I mean it's hard to, it seems so

speaker: SS
words: different.

speaker: LO
words: Uh huh. Well, it wasn't only married people, but older people just didn't

speaker: LO
words: go to college here. Of course there were only about 1800 students at

speaker: LO
words: the time I graduated and you knew a large percentage of the student body

speaker: LO
words: you knew at least by sight. But the older people that were in school--oh,

speaker: LO
words: they were largely, I have difficulty in thinkig even who they were. But

speaker: LO
words: there were a few. They were professional people come back for another

speaker: LO
words: that

speaker: LO
words: degree or something like that. But very few of them. And think too as

speaker: LO
words: far as the educational climate was concerned there was much more of the

speaker: LO
words: feeling that college was an education--something you got and that was it.

speaker: LO
words: Then you had it, you know. Whereas now I think most peqLe feel that it's

speaker: LO
words: just the beginning of education college degree, is just the beginnings

speaker: LO
words: That you go on from there to a lifetime of learning if you have any

speaker: LO
words: intellectual pretensions at all.

speaker: SS
words: I think that's probably very true, but I had never thought that it didn't

speaker: SS
words: use to be that way too. But it didn't.

speaker: LO
words: No, it didn't, and the teaching was very different. At least I found it

speaker: LO
words: so. I didn't relize quite so much at the time, but I can't remember ever

speaker: LO
words: being in a class where we were asked to form our own opinions, to evaluate

words: and look at evidence and draw conclusions from it. What we got was somebody

speaker: LO
words: else's opinion and you were supposed to learn this and ybu know, parrot it

speaker: LO
words: back on an examination.

speaker: SS
words: I wish I could say that I thought it had really had gone all the way

speaker: SS
words: the other way, but I think some courses are still that way.

speaker: LO
words: Yes, I think so too. And I think too, I thought then;that is maybe

speaker: LO
words: in retrospect, but probably some of the worst teaching in the country

speaker: LO
words: went on in colleges. For which the colleges had themselves to blame

speaker: LO
words: I think; because they don't ask you whether you can teach when you apply

speaker: LO
words: for a job they ask you what degrees you had and what you published. And

speaker: LO
words: they're not necessarily the same thing.

speaker: SS
words: Well for you, what was it that made you find that you could keep on learning

speaker: SS
words: after college. What shaped your own sense of learning? Because it

speaker: SS
words: doeh't sound like the milieu was one to particularly encourage that.

speaker: LO
words: I don't know. Of course, I went on to summer schools and things like that.

speaker: LO
words: And I picked up a couple of master's degrees later, I suppose the fact

speaker: LO
words: that I was in college teaching was it; maybe more than anything else.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: teaching yourself early in your own teaching or did it take time for you. . .

speaker: LO
words: Well, most of my teaching before I was married was in physical education,

speaker: LO
words: and it isn't just the kind of thing where you do that, you know. I mean

speaker: LO
words: you're teaching physical skills, mainly. as soon as I came back to the

speaker: LO
words: teaching of English—then I tried to. But that was a good deal later. But

speaker: LO
words: I felt as I got older that I was vastly undereducated and that I had been

speaker: LO
words: cheated by not having the kind of teaching that would for me into some

speaker: LO
words: kind of independence of thought. Because it seems to me that that is one

speaker: LO
words: of the things that educated people ought to be able to do is to hunt out

speaker: LO
words: the evidence and then evaluate it and draw some kind of conclusion from

speaker: LO
words: it. Rather than making learning just a matter of memory.

speaker: SS
words: If you were going to characterize what the learning was like when you

speaker: SS
words: were going to school here, would you say that ninety per cent of it

speaker: SS
words: was that kind of learning?

speaker: LO
words: I felt that the reason that I was able to get good grades fairly easily,

speaker: LO
words: there were two reasons: one was that I had a good memory, the other one

speaker: LO
words: was that I was able to write, that is to organize material and phrase it

speaker: LO
words: so that it was intelligible. I could write a good essay test. Sut I think

speaker: LO
words: as far as the other sort of thing, that is questioning anything that was

speaker: LO
words: handed down as the word of wisdom, you know, I didn't do it. I wasn't trained

speaker: LO
words: that way. I had to come to that later. And it seems to me that it shouldn't

speaker: LO
words: be that hard to get there.

words: teachers that you could consider outstanding teachers regardless of the

speaker: LO
words: on it, I had about one high school teacher and about one college teacher

words: that I was outstanding. That was atiut it. And when I looked back later

speaker: LO
words: had

speaker: LO
words: on at the college teacher that I felt at the time was outstanding, I decided

speaker: LO
words: that she wasn't really as outstanding as I had felt she was, it was just that

speaker: LO
words: I liked her so well. So I think that there was a great deal of really

speaker: LO
words: mediocre or aib-average teaching going on. And when I did go back to school

speaker: LO
words: the

speaker: LO
words: to work on an M.A. in English I wanted to do student teaching in high school,

speaker: LO
words: for one thing as a matter of insurance, I wanted to qualify for a secondary

speaker: LO
words: certificate. And I had never had practice teaching,and that was required.

words: And I wanted to do it 'cause I wanted to see if the high school had changed

speaker: LO
words: as much as I thought they should have in the meantime. And they had changed,

speaker: LO
words: but not that much; there was still a great deal of the same sort of teaching

speaker: LO
words: that I was used to.

words: there, the way the teacher handled the clkss? Was the# ever class discusAon,

speaker: LO

words: and so on—there was never discusssion. English literature—never discussion.

words: and acourse in Chaucer^ P^nd two courses in Shakespeare, which was twelve

words: credits out of the twenty credits for the major. And that didn't leave very

words: much. I had one semester of American Lit. Of course, at that time, I didn't

words: to be taught as college course in the twenties.

words: Oh, yes, it was pretty much the same thing. It was a survey course) and we

words: started with Nathaniel Bradford and things like that and worked up through

words: Melville and Poe and so on. ind I don't remember any thing more modern than

speaker: LO
words: about 1900 in this course.

speaker: SS
words: writing careers either in the sense of serious literature right at the timeonly

speaker: SS
words: by a minority.

speaker: LO
words: No, I don't think so either. But I had a course in Victorian. I was trying

words: to think what else I took. Oh, drama, contemporary drama  had a

speaker: LO
words: course in that.

words: methods? I mean sometimes I have felt that myself.

words: I think it was very superficial. I really do. The head of the

speaker: LO
words: English department was . interest6! in Victorian and

speaker: LO
words: Shakespeare; and the course in Old English that we had is the kind of

speaker: LO
words: course that probably now isn't taught until the graduate level. I had

speaker: LO
words: a graduate course in Old English later, in the '60's, and I think probably

speaker: LO
words: now most schools you'd get it at the graduate level rather than under

words: graduate. But it was something that every English major took and I don't

speaker: LO
words: It was about a third of the student body—there were about two men to

speaker: LO
words: six hundred out of the eigiteen hundred at that time.

speaker: LO
words: Well, in the first place I think that if a family had to chose between

speaker: LO
words: would be

speaker: LO
words: educating a son and educating a daughter that the son the one always

speaker: LO
words: who would get the education,  no, I don't think they were going here for

speaker: LO
words: finishing.

words: Education was ©f significant value feeling a rounded person for man

words: or woman then?

speaker: LO
words: Well, I think most of the women that I knew in college considered themselves

words: to be preparing for jobs. I won't say careers, but preparing for jobs I

words: think most of them. Of cmrse,aany of them married and that was the end of

speaker: LO
words: their education, too.

words: No, I would say probably teaching was the best opportunity for

words: women, but women did take things like, oh, a few women majored in

words: sciences like botany and so on. But there certainly was a distinct feeling

words: that here was a large block of jobs that women just wouldn't consider,

words: you know, like engineering and chemistry and things like that.

speaker: SS
words: do this kind of work? What it that they were incapable of understanding

speaker: SS
words: engineering and chemistry?

speaker: LO
words: I think it was partly that. I think there were a lot of people who felt

speaker: LO
words: that, fhat women were inferior in intellect. But maybe even more it was,

speaker: LO
words: well, we've never had them, you know—we've never had a woman engineer

speaker: LO
words: We've always done it the other way.

speaker: SS
words: That doesn't seem like much of to me.

speaker: LO
words: Oh, it's a very poor reason. Of course, I'have always felt that we've

speaker: LO
words: always done it the other way is aboutthe poorest reason for anything,

speaker: LO
words: you know. But you'd be surprised how much of that kind f thinking I've run

speaker: LO
words: into—not always phrased that wayv 8ut it's just an attitude of resistance

speaker: LO
words: to change. I was on a commencement committee once down at the Southern

words: Branchy and we got into a big hassle over how people were going to march in.

words: And some people thought they saw a more efficient way to do it, and it was

speaker: LO
words: finally resolved on the basis, well, we've always done it the other way.

speaker: LO
words: And I think this has been^in the attitudes toward women, has played an

speaker: LO
words: important part—resistance to change.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think the attitude of the women was when you were in college?

speaker: SS
words: I mean was there much of an awareness about this kind of inequity?

speaker: LO
words: No, I don't think there was. I think that women had been brought up where

speaker: LO
words: this climate was the thing and even though they were maybe aware, they just

speaker: LO
words: figured it was something they had to accept. I don't think there was much

speaker: LO
words: rebellion against it. Oh, with a few you got it, but I think most of us

speaker: LO
words: were conditioned enough by our upbringing that we accepted that.

speaker: LO
words: Arnd I think the climate is much better now. it's not perfect yet by any

speaker: LO
words: meansr that is there's still discrimination and women play, certainly,

speaker: LO
words: a much smaller part in the things that make the world go round than they

speaker: LO
words: deserve to, but it's much better than it was. But I think that a lot of

speaker: LO
words: it was that we had grown up surrounded by this kind of conventional

speaker: LO
words: attitude, and a lot of us didn't rebel against it as we should have. And

speaker: LO
words: I can remeber being burned up when I was on the faculty at the Southern

speaker: LO
words: Branch because the executive dean, who was the head of the school, made

speaker: LO
words: no secret of the fact that given two applicants equally well qualified,

speaker: LO
words: one a man and one a woman, he would take the man anytime. And I felt

words: this was grossly unfair. 4nd I also thought that if a woman happened to be

speaker: LO
words: interested in something like women's physical education or home economics^

speaker: LO
words: that this was to her advantage because those were jobs for which men didn't

speaker: LO
words: ordinarily compete. But I felt it was very unjust that it should be so.

speaker: LO
words: I don't recall that he ever justified it, and he was what I had regarded

speaker: LO
words: as an open minded sort of fellow. He was not an old fuddy-duddy. He was

speaker: LO
words: fairly young. Oh, I think he thought there was more stability maybe, women

speaker: LO
words: Karried and went off and left the profession, ftit beyond that I don't know.

speaker: LO
words: But he made no secret of it.

speaker: SS

words: earlier days—the women getting the vote and Ida Tarbell and people like

words: thisfwhen you were in college?

words: Not that I was aware of. I really wasn't. I knew as a matter of history

words: that there had been those struggles, and I knew that it was a matter of

speaker: LO
words: some pride that Idaho had been one of the early states to give women the

words: vote, but I really wasn't very aware of the struggle for women's rights.

speaker: LO
words: Oh, I thfflc my mother was proud of that fact. -ihat Idaho had been one of

words: the early ones to give women the vote. I don't know that it was very general,

words: but at least I was aware of it. But no, I don't think that there was

words: much of a legacy from that.

speaker: SS
words: When you were growing up, can you try to characterize how differently a

speaker: SS
words: feirl would be raised from the way a boy would be raised? I mean there are

speaker: SS
words: certainly still differences now in the way a girl or boy is raised; but as

speaker: SS
words: far as what a girl was allowed to do or attitifes about her behavior. an

speaker: SS
words: you remember how it was different for you and your brother, let's say?

speaker: LO
words: Well, as I told you before my brother was seven years older than I and that

speaker: LO
words: was enough difference that just that gap in the ages would have made quite

speaker: LO
words: a difference in the way he was treated and thevey I was. But I also remember

speaker: LO
words: that when I was in high school, I think, we had some kind of a dress-up

speaker: LO
words: business I don't think it was a

speaker: LO
words: masquerade dance,but it was some kind of costume thing—and I went dressed

speaker: LO
words: as a boy and that was the first time I had ever had pants on, I think. And

speaker: LO
words: this was regarded as sort of, well it sounds kind of silly to say daring,

speaker: LO
words: but that was really what it was. Oh, I guess I had had overalls when I was

speaker: LO
words: little and used to go out to grandfather's farm, but I can't rertl member after

speaker: LO
words: we lived in town here and I was in school that I ever had any description of

speaker: LO
words: pants. And of course when I was in college, for physical education classes

words: we wore bloomers. And I can remember when shorts came in there. There

speaker: LO
words: was quite a little discussion about that as to whf ther shorts would be

words: acceptable. And I used to go to the Campfire Girls' Camp up at

words: Cottar d'Alene Lake, both as a camper and as a counselor. And I was among

words: about that—to decide whether anklets would be permissible or not.

words: And the decision was it was all right for ordinary days, but not for visiting

speaker: LO
words: Sundays. Not when there were visitors.

speaker: SS
words: What are anklets?

speaker: LO
words: Well, just bobby socks!

speaker: SS
words: Oh, just a short little sock.

speaker: LO
words: Yeah, just short socks. Up to that time we wore knee-high socks, and this

speaker: LO
words: would have be along in the late twenties. And wearing shorts created equal

speaker: LO
words: prbblem at first. This was innovative. This was something that we've always

speaker: LO
words: done the other way, Something that hadn't been done before.

speaker: SS
words: That's funny. It's hard for me to not see this question of equality of

speaker: SS
words: the sexes as one where the men had good thing

speaker: SS
words: going and wanted to keep it that way, was really a question of,1 guess

speaker: SS
words: oppression As aword that seems to fit. I prefer things in just

speaker: SS
words: the social context than simply political, but it does seem like there's

speaker: SS
words: something pretty darned unfair.

speaker: LO
words: Yes, it was and I don't think men were consciously oppressive, I think

speaker: LO
words: this was just the way things had been.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, I suppose from what you're saying in general, there wasn't a very

speaker: SS
words: strong eense of oppression on the part of the women either. They didn't feel

speaker: SS
words: themselves oppressed.

speaker: LO
words: No, I think mostly not. I think that this was not the average response,

speaker: LO
words: certainly. It was the response of afew, but not everybody. And I think

speaker: LO
words: the men in a sense were the victims of the climate, too, because they

speaker: LO
words: accepted without questioning these inequities^because it never occurred

speaker: LO
words: to them that it ought to be any different.

speaker: LO
words: and

speaker: SS
words: You know, if you try to think of your parents other adults that

speaker: SS
words: you know, actually think ebout the relationships of older people. Do

speaker: SS
words: you think that they were relationships of inequality

speaker: SS
words: and not ones of equality? I'm not sure how we can define what equality

speaker: SS
words: really means^and that's pant of the difficulty, but I'm trying to think

speaker: SS
words: of it from the other side- in my family
